original_abstract:
On February 6, 2023, Türkiye faced one of the most devastating earthquake disasters in its history, with ten provinces suffering immense destruction. In the immediate aftermath, the Ministry of National Education (MoNE) mobilized its extensive human resources and production capacity to provide both humanitarian aid and essential education services. This study evaluates the MoNE’s multi-faceted response through the lens of global frameworks for education in emergencies, such as those established by INEE and UNESCO-IIEP. 

Findings reveal that MoNE institutions became central hubs for disaster relief, taking the lead in producing and distributing food, shelter materials, and heating supplies. To restore a sense of normalcy for children, the Ministry rapidly established learning environments using tents, containers, and prefabricated schools, while deploying a large force of teachers and psychological counselors to address the community’s trauma. Specialized initiatives, such as "hospital classes" for injured students and streamlined transfer processes for those relocating to other cities, ensured that education remained accessible despite the displacement. Furthermore, the MoNE utilized its prior experience from the COVID-19 pandemic to manage large-scale social assistance effectively. The study concludes that the MoNE’s policy actions aligned with international best practices, demonstrating how a national education system can serve as a vital pillar of resilience during a major humanitarian crisis.
humanized_abstract:
In February 6, 2023, Türkiye was struck by the devastating 6.8 and 7.5 magnitude earthquake disasters in ten provinces. In the first weeks after the disaster, the Ministry of National Education (MoNE) made the most of its size and potential by making the maximum efforts for humanitarian aid and the continuation of education activities. This study provides a critique of the Ministry of National Education’s multiple functions, approaches and actions in the context of its educational responses to the earthquake disaster as an EER through INEE and UNESCO-IIEP frameworks.

Data shows that the educational institutions affiliated with the MoNE have become focal points of the disaster relief work. Hence, they started to produce and distribute foodstuff, building materials and heating stuff inside the country. With this aim, the Ministry immediately established schools inside tents, containers and modular buildings and sent thousands of educators and psychological counsellors to the affected regions. In addition, hospital classes were established in hospitals to serve the students who were injured in the Earthquake, and the students who wanted to move to other cities had their registration and admission process facilitated by the Ministry. Moreover, the Ministry benefited from the experiences gained during the Corona period in the implementation of the social aid programmes within the scope of the ministry’s current disaster relief activities. In general, it has been observed that the policy actions of the MoNE are in line with the international best practices as a national education system responds to a massive humanitarian disaster while being a basic element of the society’s response and resilience to disasters.
